romania.summary: Cocalarii bucureșteni s-au revoltat după ce Primăria a anunțat că vrea să cumpere în jur de 100 de tramvaie noi. Ei spun că și în prezent condițiile de trafic sunt dificile, însă cu încă 100 de tramvaie pe șine ele vor deveni de-a dreptul dezastruoase. „În mod normal ar trebui eliminate din tramvaiele care circulă […] The post Cocalarii cu BMW, revoltați că PMB vrea să cumpere 100 de tramvaie: „Abia avem noi loc pe șine!” appeared first on Times New Roman .

La nivel global, compania investește peste 24% din veniturile sale în R&D. Astfel, în anul 2024 suma a ajuns …

romania.summary: Sătui să aştepte după noi, bulgarii apasă pedala de acceleraţie şi ne depăşesc fără drept de apel! După ce au reuşit anul acesta să adopte euro şi să câştige Eurovisionul înaintea noastră, reprezentanţii Sofiei anunţă că au semnat chiar în această dimineaţă unirea cu Republica Moldova. „Am discutat îndelung cu doamna Maia Sandu şi am […] The post Iar ne-o iau înainte! Fanii felinelor de apartament nu trebuie încă să se entuziasmeze […] The post Un român a creat o pisică inteligentă care golește singură litiera, dar o golește în ciorbă appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

medical.title: ESTRO: Select breast cancer patients may be able to omit surgery following ablative radiation
medical.summary: A select group of patients with early-stage breast cancer were able to omit surgery with no tumor progression after three years, according to results of a Phase 2 trial of ablative radiation therapy and endocrine therapy led by researchers at The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center.

medical.title: Poor sleep tied to Alzheimer's risk in older women
medical.summary: Sleep complaints may be an important Alzheimer's disease risk factor in older women with a higher genetic predisposition to the disease, according to a new study from researchers at the University of California San Diego. The findings suggest that women with a higher genetic risk for Alzheimer's who reported poorer sleep also showed greater memory difficulties and more Alzheimer's-related brain changes.

medical.title: Inside the brain's memory gate: 50,000 cryo-EM 'movies' reveal a hidden ion filter with high stakes for brain developmen
medical.summary: How do we learn to remember? At the most fundamental level, it's all about chemicals and electricity. Beyond their roles in diet and nutrition, calcium and magnesium work as ions, or charged particles, in the brain. Magnesium can block a channel found within brain receptors known as NMDARs. When the blockade lifts, calcium can pass through the channel. These processes enable the brain to perform essential functions, like learning and remembering.

medical.title: Healthy eating improves memory but sugary diets may cause lasting damage, animal study finds
medical.summary: As concern grows about the long-term health effects of modern diets, new research led by the University of Technology Sydney (UTS) has examined how changes in what we eat affect memory and brain function.

medical.title: Researchers discover new clues to delaying type 1 diabetes
medical.summary: Thomas Delong, Ph.D., moved to Colorado from Germany two decades ago intent on one thing: understanding the origin of type 1 diabetes (T1D) and finding ways to stop it. Diagnosed at age 12 with the disease that affects 9.5 million people worldwide, Delong's mission was deep-rooted and personal.

medical.title: Clinical trial shows tezepelumab controls asthma in diverse patient population
medical.summary: The drug tezepelumab was shown to significantly reduce exacerbations in people with severe asthma in clinical trials. Now, a new study presented at the 2026 ATS International Conference shows that it is also safe and effective in real-world patients.

medical.title: Kids with chronic stomach pain got relief when treatment changed one crucial lesson about their bodies
medical.summary: Chronic abdominal pain affects an estimated 10%–15% of children and is a leading cause of school absence and daily disruption for families. For many children, the experience does more than hurt—it teaches them something potentially harmful: that their bodies cannot be trusted. A new study by researchers at Duke University challenges that assumption, suggesting that how children learn to relate to their bodies may be just as important as how their symptoms are treated.

medical.title: Machine learning reveals common genetic cause behind unexplained peripheral neuropathy
medical.summary: Researchers at WashU Medicine and collaborating institutions have developed a novel computational tool that can accurately identify a genetic problem in a gene called RFC1 that is linked to certain forms of peripheral neuropathy. Peripheral neuropathy is one of the most common neurological disorders and can cause pain, sensory loss, imbalance and weakness. It affects 12%–20% of all people in the U.S. and can affect up to 30% of adults over age 65. The new research is published in the Annals of Neurology.

medical.title: Once-nightly pill treats causes of airway collapse to control obstructive sleep apnea in large clinical trial
medical.summary: A once-nightly oral pill helped control obstructive sleep apnea in a large, Phase III clinical trial presented at the 2026 ATS International Conference. The drug, called AD109, is the first therapy to treat OSA by addressing its underlying mechanisms and targeting the neuromuscular causes of airway collapse.

medical.title: Monet for nothing: the famous artworks cheering up UK high streets
medical.summary: Reproductions of works by Monet, Picasso and Van Gogh are being hung in town centres to bring art closer to communities The post Monet for nothing: the famous artworks cheering up UK high streets appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: A common constipation drug shows surprising power to protect kidneys
medical.summary: A common constipation drug may have unexpectedly unlocked a new way to slow chronic kidney disease — a condition that affects millions and often leads to dialysis. In a clinical trial involving 150 patients, researchers found that lubiprostone, a medication normally used to treat constipation, helped preserve kidney function in people with moderate CKD. Scientists traced the effect to changes in gut bacteria that boosted production of spermidine, a compound linked to healthier mitochondria and reduced kidney damage.

medical.title: Scientists discover a new way to prevent gum disease without killing good bacteria
medical.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising way to influence the bacteria living in our mouths — not by killing them, but by interrupting how they “talk” to each other. Researchers found that dental plaque bacteria use chemical signals to coordinate growth, and by blocking those signals, they were able to encourage healthier bacteria while reducing disease-linked microbes tied to gum disease. Even more intriguing, the bacterial conversations changed depending on oxygen levels above and below the gums, revealing an entirely new layer of complexity inside the mouth.

medical.title: 15 of the best quotes from Sir David Attenborough
medical.summary: On his 100th birthday, we share some of our favourite quotes from the broadcaster and natural historian The post 15 of the best quotes from Sir David Attenborough appeared first on Positive News .

science.title: This single mother must learn quickly—or her colony won't survive
science.summary: Being a single mother of 20 is no joke, especially if the survival of a whole species depends on it. A queen bumblebee faces this very challenge when she lays her first eggs in the spring: She is utterly alone, with no worker bees to help.

science.title: Machine learning reveals 5-angstrom sweet spot behind metallic glass stability
science.summary: Using the second-nearest neighboring atoms to predict metallic glass stability can help researchers more accurately model the disordered solid with strong, elastic properties, according to a recent study led by University of Michigan Engineering researchers.

science.title: New evidence reveals a millennium-old dingo was ritually buried, and cared for, in Australia
science.summary: A millennium-old dingo deliberately buried by Barkindji ancestors along the Baaka, or Darling River, is offering rare insight into the depth of relationships between First Nations people and dingoes in western New South Wales, Australia.

science.title: Antarctic DNA offers vital clues to pinpointing rising sea levels
science.summary: Researchers say accurately predicting Antarctica's impact on global sea levels is an urgent priority that can be achieved by analyzing the DNA of tiny land animals, pinpointing the continent's icy past to paint a clearer picture of the future.

science.title: Policing plagiarism of ideas in generative AI-assisted research writing
science.summary: As more people—including researchers—use generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) in their writing, it's becoming increasingly important to define what plagiarism looks like and how to police it.

science.title: Behold the neuron, a complicated cell with a simple mission
science.summary: Neurons, the uber-connected nerve cells that act as a main switchboard for the brain, are central to some incredibly complicated processes. They make it possible to think, walk, speak, and breathe. They even have built-in backup batteries to use in emergencies.

science.title: How climate change is affecting water demand in Scotland
science.summary: The volume of water drawn from Scotland's rivers and lochs by the agricultural sector surged by more than 500% during periods of water scarcity in recent years, new research has found. The University of Strathclyde study examined the monthly and annual water use records of more than 80 of the sectors which use most water, including aquaculture, food and drink production, oil refining and paper manufacturing. It identified patterns influencing water use in weather extremes, changes in the economy and industries adapting their practices.

science.title: Hidden clean energy under mountains? Why erosion could shape hydrogen prospects in Alps and Pyrenees
science.summary: Hydrogen gas formed by natural processes in the subsurface of mountain ranges could represent a promising source of clean energy. A new international study led by Unil and GFZ shows that erosion plays a key and complex role in the formation and accumulation of this natural resource. The research confirms that the Pyrenees and the Alps could constitute key targets for natural hydrogen exploration.

science.title: Images: NASA's Perseverance captures panorama at 'Arbot'
science.summary: NASA's Perseverance Mars rover used its Mastcam-Z camera to capture this panorama of an area nicknamed "Arbot" on April 5, 2026, the 1,882nd Martian day (sol) of the mission, during the rover's deepest push west beyond Jezero Crater.

science.title: Twisted WSe₂ reveals elusive charge-neutral quantum modes
science.summary: Quantum materials, materials with properties that are influenced by the laws of quantum mechanics, have attracted considerable attention over the past few decades. Their unique properties make these materials advantageous for the development of numerous cutting-edge technologies, including quantum computers, highly sensitive sensors and energy-efficient electronics.

science.title: Smart soil design to stop Australia's underground water pipelines from corroding
science.summary: Engineers are rethinking the soil surrounding buried pipelines in a bid to help reduce Australia's billion-dollar annual corrosion problem and extend the lifespan of critical infrastructure. The Monash research, published in Geotechnical and Geological Engineering, explores how specially designed backfill materials could be used as an active corrosion protection system rather than simply providing mechanical support.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.summary: 23 de scurtmetraje de pe 3 continente se află în cursa pentru cele trei premii, în valoare totală de 5.500 de euro, acordate în cadrul celor două secțiuni competiționale ale… Articolul Competițiile ESTE FILM Festival aduc la Sibiu noile voci ale cinemaului: 23 de scurtmetraje de pe 3 continente apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.summary: Duminică, 17 mai 2026, plaja Zoom Beach din Constanța a găzduit ediția a XI-a a Maratonului Nisipului, eveniment care a reunit mii de participanți într-o zi dedicată sportului, mișcării și… Articolul Ploaia nu i-a oprit: mii de participanți la ediția aniversară a Maratonului Nisipului. Câștigătorii ediției 2026: spaniolul Jesús Lorenzo Rubiales și Irina Popa s-au impus la maraton apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Iar ne-o iau înainte! După euro şi victoria la Eurovision, bulgarii anunţă că se unesc cu Moldova
mix_items.summary: Sătui să aştepte după noi, bulgarii apasă pedala de acceleraţie şi ne depăşesc fără drept de apel! După ce au reuşit anul acesta să adopte euro şi să câştige Eurovisionul înaintea noastră, reprezentanţii Sofiei anunţă că au semnat chiar în această dimineaţă unirea cu Republica Moldova. „Am discutat îndelung cu doamna Maia Sandu şi am […] The post Iar ne-o iau înainte! După euro şi victoria la Eurovision, bulgarii anunţă că se unesc cu Moldova appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: Interes record pentru baletul clasic la Bucureşti: compania Ukrainian Classical Ballet, care a umplut Sala Palatului până la refuz cu „Lacul lebedelor” la început de an, va pune în scenă… Articolul Două reprezentaţii cu SPĂRGĂTORUL DE NUCI, în aceeași zi, de Sfântul Andrei – pe 30 noiembrie – la Bucureşti apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Un român a creat o pisică inteligentă care golește singură litiera, dar o golește în ciorbă
mix_items.summary: După 40 de ani de împerecheri controlate și o selecție atentă a genelor, un savant român din Focșani a reușit să creeze o pisică extrem de inteligentă și de bine dresată, care nu doar că își face nevoile la litieră, dar o și golește singură. Fanii felinelor de apartament nu trebuie încă să se entuziasmeze […] The post Un român a creat o pisică inteligentă care golește singură litiera, dar o golește în ciorbă appeared first on Times New Roman .

Articolul Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: ESTRO: Select breast cancer patients may be able to omit surgery following ablative radiation
mix_items.summary: A select group of patients with early-stage breast cancer were able to omit surgery with no tumor progression after three years, according to results of a Phase 2 trial of ablative radiation therapy and endocrine therapy led by researchers at The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center.

mix_items.title: Cum poate deveni grădina sală de clasă
mix_items.summary: EduGrădina este un program integrat gândit pentru clasele primare și construit să funcționeze alături de curriculumul oficial, nu în locul lui. Elevii plantează, observă, recoltează, compară,...

mix_items.summary: Alexandru are 10 ani, locuiește într-un sat din județul Giurgiu și este elev în clasa a III‑a. Este un copil cu... Articolul Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: This single mother must learn quickly—or her colony won't survive
mix_items.summary: Being a single mother of 20 is no joke, especially if the survival of a whole species depends on it. A queen bumblebee faces this very challenge when she lays her first eggs in the spring: She is utterly alone, with no worker bees to help.

mix_items.title: SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025
mix_items.summary: Programul național Telefonul Vârstnicului – 0800 460 001 Sprijinul media a venit de la Spot TV – Pro TV, TVR, Antena 1, Antena 3 CNN, Antena Stars, Prima TV, Prima News, Pro Cinema, Pro Arena, Acasă, Acasă Gold, Trinitas TV, Speranța TV, Antena 3 Deva,... mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Machine learning reveals 5-angstrom sweet spot behind metallic glass stability
mix_items.summary: Using the second-nearest neighboring atoms to predict metallic glass stability can help researchers more accurately model the disordered solid with strong, elastic properties, according to a recent study led by University of Michigan Engineering researchers.

mix_items.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: București, aprilie 2026 – Într-o lume în care cererea de hrană crește accelerat, iar presiunea asupra resurselor naturale este tot... Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: New evidence reveals a millennium-old dingo was ritually buried, and cared for, in Australia
mix_items.summary: A millennium-old dingo deliberately buried by Barkindji ancestors along the Baaka, or Darling River, is offering rare insight into the depth of relationships between First Nations people and dingoes in western New South Wales, Australia.

mix_items.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase.
mix_items.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Antarctic DNA offers vital clues to pinpointing rising sea levels
mix_items.summary: Researchers say accurately predicting Antarctica's impact on global sea levels is an urgent priority that can be achieved by analyzing the DNA of tiny land animals, pinpointing the continent's icy past to paint a clearer picture of the future.

mix_items.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
mix_items.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Poor sleep tied to Alzheimer's risk in older women
mix_items.summary: Sleep complaints may be an important Alzheimer's disease risk factor in older women with a higher genetic predisposition to the disease, according to a new study from researchers at the University of California San Diego. The findings suggest that women with a higher genetic risk for Alzheimer's who reported poorer sleep also showed greater memory difficulties and more Alzheimer's-related brain changes.

mix_items.title: Inside the brain's memory gate: 50,000 cryo-EM 'movies' reveal a hidden ion filter with high stakes for brain developmen
mix_items.summary: How do we learn to remember? At the most fundamental level, it's all about chemicals and electricity. Beyond their roles in diet and nutrition, calcium and magnesium work as ions, or charged particles, in the brain. Magnesium can block a channel found within brain receptors known as NMDARs. When the blockade lifts, calcium can pass through the channel. These processes enable the brain to perform essential functions, like learning and remembering.

mix_items.title: Policing plagiarism of ideas in generative AI-assisted research writing
mix_items.summary: As more people—including researchers—use generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) in their writing, it's becoming increasingly important to define what plagiarism looks like and how to police it.

mix_items.title: Healthy eating improves memory but sugary diets may cause lasting damage, animal study finds
mix_items.summary: As concern grows about the long-term health effects of modern diets, new research led by the University of Technology Sydney (UTS) has examined how changes in what we eat affect memory and brain function.

mix_items.title: Researchers discover new clues to delaying type 1 diabetes
mix_items.summary: Thomas Delong, Ph.D., moved to Colorado from Germany two decades ago intent on one thing: understanding the origin of type 1 diabetes (T1D) and finding ways to stop it. Diagnosed at age 12 with the disease that affects 9.5 million people worldwide, Delong's mission was deep-rooted and personal.

mix_items.title: Behold the neuron, a complicated cell with a simple mission
mix_items.summary: Neurons, the uber-connected nerve cells that act as a main switchboard for the brain, are central to some incredibly complicated processes. They make it possible to think, walk, speak, and breathe. They even have built-in backup batteries to use in emergencies.

mix_items.title: How climate change is affecting water demand in Scotland
mix_items.summary: The volume of water drawn from Scotland's rivers and lochs by the agricultural sector surged by more than 500% during periods of water scarcity in recent years, new research has found. The University of Strathclyde study examined the monthly and annual water use records of more than 80 of the sectors which use most water, including aquaculture, food and drink production, oil refining and paper manufacturing. It identified patterns influencing water use in weather extremes, changes in the economy and industries adapting their practices.

mix_items.title: Clinical trial shows tezepelumab controls asthma in diverse patient population
mix_items.summary: The drug tezepelumab was shown to significantly reduce exacerbations in people with severe asthma in clinical trials. Now, a new study presented at the 2026 ATS International Conference shows that it is also safe and effective in real-world patients.

mix_items.title: Hidden clean energy under mountains? Why erosion could shape hydrogen prospects in Alps and Pyrenees
mix_items.summary: Hydrogen gas formed by natural processes in the subsurface of mountain ranges could represent a promising source of clean energy. A new international study led by Unil and GFZ shows that erosion plays a key and complex role in the formation and accumulation of this natural resource. The research confirms that the Pyrenees and the Alps could constitute key targets for natural hydrogen exploration.

mix_items.title: Kids with chronic stomach pain got relief when treatment changed one crucial lesson about their bodies
mix_items.summary: Chronic abdominal pain affects an estimated 10%–15% of children and is a leading cause of school absence and daily disruption for families. For many children, the experience does more than hurt—it teaches them something potentially harmful: that their bodies cannot be trusted. A new study by researchers at Duke University challenges that assumption, suggesting that how children learn to relate to their bodies may be just as important as how their symptoms are treated.

mix_items.title: Machine learning reveals common genetic cause behind unexplained peripheral neuropathy
mix_items.summary: Researchers at WashU Medicine and collaborating institutions have developed a novel computational tool that can accurately identify a genetic problem in a gene called RFC1 that is linked to certain forms of peripheral neuropathy. Peripheral neuropathy is one of the most common neurological disorders and can cause pain, sensory loss, imbalance and weakness. It affects 12%–20% of all people in the U.S. and can affect up to 30% of adults over age 65. The new research is published in the Annals of Neurology.

mix_items.title: Images: NASA's Perseverance captures panorama at 'Arbot'
mix_items.summary: NASA's Perseverance Mars rover used its Mastcam-Z camera to capture this panorama of an area nicknamed "Arbot" on April 5, 2026, the 1,882nd Martian day (sol) of the mission, during the rover's deepest push west beyond Jezero Crater.

mix_items.title: Twisted WSe₂ reveals elusive charge-neutral quantum modes
mix_items.summary: Quantum materials, materials with properties that are influenced by the laws of quantum mechanics, have attracted considerable attention over the past few decades. Their unique properties make these materials advantageous for the development of numerous cutting-edge technologies, including quantum computers, highly sensitive sensors and energy-efficient electronics.

mix_items.title: Smart soil design to stop Australia's underground water pipelines from corroding
mix_items.summary: Engineers are rethinking the soil surrounding buried pipelines in a bid to help reduce Australia's billion-dollar annual corrosion problem and extend the lifespan of critical infrastructure. The Monash research, published in Geotechnical and Geological Engineering, explores how specially designed backfill materials could be used as an active corrosion protection system rather than simply providing mechanical support.

mix_items.title: Once-nightly pill treats causes of airway collapse to control obstructive sleep apnea in large clinical trial
mix_items.summary: A once-nightly oral pill helped control obstructive sleep apnea in a large, Phase III clinical trial presented at the 2026 ATS International Conference. The drug, called AD109, is the first therapy to treat OSA by addressing its underlying mechanisms and targeting the neuromuscular causes of airway collapse.

mix_items.title: Monet for nothing: the famous artworks cheering up UK high streets
mix_items.summary: Reproductions of works by Monet, Picasso and Van Gogh are being hung in town centres to bring art closer to communities The post Monet for nothing: the famous artworks cheering up UK high streets appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: A common constipation drug shows surprising power to protect kidneys
mix_items.summary: A common constipation drug may have unexpectedly unlocked a new way to slow chronic kidney disease — a condition that affects millions and often leads to dialysis. In a clinical trial involving 150 patients, researchers found that lubiprostone, a medication normally used to treat constipation, helped preserve kidney function in people with moderate CKD. Scientists traced the effect to changes in gut bacteria that boosted production of spermidine, a compound linked to healthier mitochondria and reduced kidney damage.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover a new way to prevent gum disease without killing good bacteria
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising way to influence the bacteria living in our mouths — not by killing them, but by interrupting how they “talk” to each other. Researchers found that dental plaque bacteria use chemical signals to coordinate growth, and by blocking those signals, they were able to encourage healthier bacteria while reducing disease-linked microbes tied to gum disease. Even more intriguing, the bacterial conversations changed depending on oxygen levels above and below the gums, revealing an entirely new layer of complexity inside the mouth.

mix_items.title: 15 of the best quotes from Sir David Attenborough
mix_items.summary: On his 100th birthday, we share some of our favourite quotes from the broadcaster and natural historian The post 15 of the best quotes from Sir David Attenborough appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Ce nivel de motivatie ai azi? - Decorativ.

satire_ro.title: Cocalarii cu BMW, revoltați că PMB vrea să cumpere 100 de tramvaie: „Abia avem noi loc pe șine!”
